title: Fury vs Joshua: the all-British heavyweight fight a decade in the making
summary: After years of near-misses, Tyson Fury and Anthony Joshua are finally being matched. Here is how British boxing's two biggest heavyweights compare on the numbers.
body:
It has been talked about for the better part of a decade, and now the numbers can finally sit side by side. Tyson Fury and Anthony Joshua are the two names that have defined British heavyweight boxing for a generation, and a meeting between them is the biggest domestic fight the division has to offer. They arrive as very different fighters.

Tyson Fury

Fury's record stands at 35-2-1. What sets him apart is how he wins. Only 24 of those victories have come by knockout, with 11 going to the cards, the profile of a boxer who controls range and tempo rather than hunting the finish. At 6ft 9in with an 85-inch reach he is the bigger man here, and that length has always been central to how he operates. His recent five is the honest part of the story, carrying two defeats among the wins, so he comes in with real questions about where he sits in his career.

Anthony Joshua

Joshua is the puncher. His 29-4 record includes 26 stoppages, a knockout rate around 90 per cent, and just three wins on the cards. At 6ft 6in with an 82-inch reach he gives up three inches of height and reach to Fury, but he carries the heavier hands by a clear margin. His own recent form is mixed, a run of knockout wins broken up by a stoppage defeat, so like Fury he comes to this past the smoothest point of his career.

How they match up

  • Size: Fury is 6ft 9in to Joshua's 6ft 6in, with a three-inch reach advantage (85in to 82).
  • Finishing: Joshua stops opponents far more often, around 90 per cent of his wins by knockout against Fury's 69.
  • Going the distance: Fury has 11 decision wins to Joshua's three, the mark of a fighter comfortable boxing long.
  • Experience: Fury has had 38 professional contests to Joshua's 33, and both are deep into their thirties.

What to watch

The shape of it is clear from the figures. Fury will want to use his size, reach and movement to keep the fight at distance and turn it into a boxing match over twelve rounds, where his decision record lives. Joshua will want the opposite, to close the gap and land the heavy single shots that have ended most of his fights. Both men carry recent defeats, which is the part the records do not let either side hide, and it is exactly that shared vulnerability that makes a fight avoided for so long finally feel worth making.
